title: Hospitality, Diotrephes, and Demetrius
subtitle: The elder commends Gaius, rebukes Diotrephes, and endorses Demetrius
prayer_prompt: As John commends Gaius for his hospitality and rebukes Diotrephes for his pride, consider asking God whether you've been more of a Gaius or a Diotrephes lately — opening doors or loving to be first.

heb.paragraph: The elder to the beloved Gaius - the term of affection occurs four times in this short letter. Gaius is genuinely loved.

heb.gloss: to prosper, succeed, have a good journey
heb.paragraph: I pray that all may go well with you and that you may be in good health - a conventional greeting elevated by genuine pastoral concern.
You are faithful in what you are doing for the brothers, even though they are strangers - hospitality to itinerant missionaries is commended.

refs.note: Gaius, who is host to me and to the whole church, greets you.
I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth.
Whoever receives you receives me; whoever gives a cup of cold water...
Do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers.

notes.note: The elder to beloved Gaius. I pray all goes well with you and your health, as it goes well with your soul. I rejoice that you walk in truth.
You are faithful in serving the brothers, even strangers. Send them on worthily. They went out for the name, accepting nothing from Gentiles. We ought to support them.

Calvin notes the prayer for physical health alongside spiritual health. The elder cares for the whole person. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: As it goes well with your soul - the prayer assumes spiritual prosperity and asks that physical well-being match it. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

For the sake of the name (hyper tou onomatos) - the Name is Jesus. Early Christians went out as his representatives. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

yarbrough.source: Robert Yarbrough, BECNT 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Yarbrough emphasizes that hospitality to missionaries was essential for gospel advance. Gaius faithful service enabled their ministry. Yarbrough traces the epistle's logic through its characteristic contrasts: light and darkness, truth and falsehood, love and hate. The author's binary rhetoric is not simplistic but diagnostic — it exposes the fundamental orientation of the heart and calls readers to genuine self-examination in the light of God's revealed character.

kruse.source: Colin Kruse, TNTC/Pillar 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kruse notes that accepting nothing from the Gentiles maintained the integrity of the mission. Financial independence prevented accusations of greed. Kruse's analysis focuses on the letter's pastoral function within its historical context. The author addresses concrete community concerns — false teaching, relational fracture, assurance of salvation — with theological precision that grounds pastoral care in the foundational realities of the incarnation and atonement.

hist.historical: Third John is a personal letter from 'the elder' to Gaius, commending his hospitality to traveling missionaries. The letter reveals conflict within the early church: Diotrephes 'loves to be first' (v. 9), refuses to acknowledge the elder's authority, spreads malicious accusations, and excommunicates those who show hospitality to the elder's emissaries. This power struggle illustrates the transition from apostolic to local leadership in the late first century. Demetrius is commended as trustworthy (v. 12), possibly the letter carrier. The letter provides a rare window into the practical challenges of church governance, hospitality networks, and authority disputes in early Christianity.
hist.context: The elder to the beloved Gaius, whom I love in truth. Beloved, I pray that all may go well with you and that you may be in good health, as it goes well with your soul. For I rejoiced greatly when the brothers came and testified to your truth, as indeed you are walking in the truth. I have no greater joy than to hear that my children are walking in the truth. Beloved, it is a faithful thing you do in all your efforts for these brothers, strangers as they are, who testified to your love before the church. You will do well to send them on their journey in a manner worthy of God. For they have gone out for the sake of the name, accepting nothing from the Gentiles. Therefore we ought to support people like these, that we may be fellow workers for the truth.

heb.gloss: to love to be first, desire preeminence
heb.paragraph: Diotrephes, who likes to put himself first - the compound word describes ambition for supremacy. He loves being number one.

heb.gloss: to cast out, expel, drive out
heb.paragraph: He even puts them out of the church - Diotrephes excommunicates those who show hospitality. He usurps authority.
Demetrius has received a good testimony from everyone - universal commendation contrasts with Diotrephes condemnation.

refs.note: They love the place of honor at feasts and the best seats.
Whoever would be great among you must be your servant.
Men will arise, speaking twisted things, to draw away disciples.
Do not admit a charge against an elder except on the evidence of two or three witnesses.

notes.note: Diotrephes loves to be first and does not acknowledge our authority. He talks wicked nonsense, refuses to welcome brothers, and expels those who do.
Do not imitate evil but good. Whoever does good is from God. Demetrius has good testimony from everyone, from truth itself, and from us.

The contrast between Diotrephes and Demetrius is stark: one seeks self-glory, the other receives universal commendation. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Philoproteuo (loves to be first) is a rare compound verb. Diotrephes craves position and recognition. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

The truth itself testifies to Demetrius - his life aligns with the gospel. Character and message match. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

yarbrough.source: Robert Yarbrough, BECNT 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Yarbrough notes that Diotrephes represents a recurring problem: leaders who prioritize personal power over gospel mission. Yarbrough traces the epistle's logic through its characteristic contrasts: light and darkness, truth and falsehood, love and hate. The author's binary rhetoric is not simplistic but diagnostic — it exposes the fundamental orientation of the heart and calls readers to genuine self-examination in the light of God's revealed character.

kruse.source: Colin Kruse, TNTC/Pillar 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kruse emphasizes that the elder plans to confront Diotrephes personally. Serious problems require direct address. Kruse's analysis focuses on the letter's pastoral function within its historical context. The author addresses concrete community concerns — false teaching, relational fracture, assurance of salvation — with theological precision that grounds pastoral care in the foundational realities of the incarnation and atonement.

hist.context: I have written something to the church, but Diotrephes, who likes to put himself first, does not acknowledge our authority. So if I come, I will bring up what he is doing, talking wicked nonsense against us. And not content with that, he refuses to welcome the brothers, and also stops those who want to and puts them out of the church. Beloved, do not imitate evil but imitate good. Whoever does good is from God; whoever does evil has not seen God. Demetrius has received a good testimony from everyone, and from the truth itself. We also add our testimony, and you know that our testimony is true.

heb.paragraph: I have much to write to you but do not want to write with pen and ink - the preference for face-to-face conversation echoes 2 John 12.
Peace be to you - the benediction of shalom. Peace with God and peace in community.
Greet the friends by name - personal attention to individuals in the community. Each is known and valued.

refs.note: I have much to write but prefer to come and talk face to face.
I have called you friends, for all that I have heard from my Father I have made known to you.
Greet every saint in Christ Jesus.

notes.note: I have much to write but prefer face-to-face conversation. Peace be to you. The friends greet you. Greet the friends by name. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Greet the friends by name (kat onoma) - the elder knows individuals personally. The church is not anonymous. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

kruse.source: Colin Kruse, TNTC/Pillar 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kruse emphasizes the personal touch: each friend is to be greeted by name. Individual attention reflects love. Kruse's analysis focuses on the letter's pastoral function within its historical context. The author addresses concrete community concerns — false teaching, relational fracture, assurance of salvation — with theological precision that grounds pastoral care in the foundational realities of the incarnation and atonement.

hist.context: I had much to write to you, but I would rather not write with pen and ink. I hope to see you soon, and we will talk face to face. Peace be to you. The friends greet you. Greet the friends, each by name.

notes.note: Calvin notes the elder's preference for personal presence over written communication — 'I have much to write to you, but I would rather not write with pen and ink.' This reflects the relational nature of early Christian fellowship. Letters were necessary but insufficient; the church thrived on face-to-face communion.

The phrase 'talk face to face' (literally 'mouth to mouth') echoes Numbers 12:8, where God speaks with Moses directly. Calvin sees here a pastoral longing: some matters require personal presence, tone of voice, the ability to read reactions and adjust. The apostolic ministry was incarnational, not merely textual.

Calvin observes the warmth of the closing: 'Peace to you. The friends greet you. Greet the friends by name.' The use of 'friends' (philoi) rather than 'brothers' may reflect local usage or the elder's affection. The instruction to greet 'by name' emphasizes the personal nature of Christian community — no one is anonymous.

yarbrough.source: Robert Yarbrough, BECNT Letters of John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Yarbrough notes that this closing closely parallels 2 John 12. The 'many things' left unwritten likely include practical matters about the traveling missionaries and the Diotrephes situation. The elder's restraint — preferring conversation to correspondence — models pastoral wisdom: some conflicts are better resolved in person.

Yarbrough emphasizes the phrase 'face to face' (stoma pros stoma): the elder values direct encounter over mediated communication. In a context where false teachers circulated letters (and perhaps forged them), personal presence authenticated both message and messenger. Gaius will know the elder when he sees him.

Yarbrough observes that 'Peace' (eirēnē) serves as both greeting and blessing — the Hebrew shalom underlying the Greek. The mutual greetings ('friends greet you... greet the friends') create a web of relationship spanning geography. The church is not isolated congregations but a connected fellowship.

themes.note: Mission dominates through the hospitality theme. Hospitality to missionaries advances the gospel. Justice appears in the contrast between Diotrephes and Demetrius.

rows.label: vv. 9-12
rows.text: Rebuke of Diotrephes; commendation of Demetrius

lit.note: 3 John is a personal letter addressing hospitality and leadership. Gaius is commended, Diotrephes is rebuked, Demetrius is endorsed.

discourse.title: 3 John — Hospitality Commended, Diotrephes Exposed, Demetrius Endorsed
discourse.thesis: The elder commends Gaius for supporting traveling Christian missionaries, rebukes Diotrephes for refusing them and excluding those who welcome them, and endorses Demetrius as trustworthy.

nodes.label: Greeting and prayer for Gaius (vv.1-4)
nodes.text: The elder to the beloved Gaius, whom he loves in truth. Praying for Gaius's prosperity and health as his soul prospers. The elder rejoiced greatly when brothers came and testified to Gaius's truth — his walking in truth. No greater joy than to hear his children walking in truth.

nodes.label: Commendation of Gaius's hospitality (vv.5-8)
nodes.text: Gaius acts faithfully whenever he serves the brothers — even strangers. They have testified to Gaius's love before the church. He will do well to send them on their way worthy of God. For they went out for the sake of the Name, accepting nothing from pagans. We ought to support such people, that we may be fellow workers in the truth.

nodes.label: Rebuke of Diotrephes (vv.9-11)
nodes.text: The elder wrote to the church, but Diotrephes — who loves to be first — does not acknowledge us. So if I come, I will bring up what he is doing: spreading malicious nonsense, refusing to welcome the brothers, stopping those who want to, and putting them out of the church. Beloved, do not imitate evil but imitate good. The one who does good is from God; the one who does evil has not seen God.

nodes.label: Endorsement of Demetrius and closing (vv.12-15)
nodes.text: Demetrius is well spoken of by everyone — and by the truth itself. We also speak well of him, and you know our testimony is true. The elder had much to write but prefers to see Gaius in person. Peace to you. The friends here send greetings. Greet the friends there by name.

discourse.note: 3 John's 15 verses preserve the NT's most concrete glimpse of itinerant-missionary economics in the late first century. Traveling Christian teachers depended on hospitality from settled house-churches; refusal to receive them (Diotrephes's fault) was both theologically and practically destructive. The letter also witnesses a local-power conflict: Diotrephes may be the first documented Christian bishop-figure refusing wider apostolic authority.

hebtext.note: v.9's hapax legomenon (unique-to-Scripture) compound noun for Diotrephes's defining flaw. The word denotes status-craving — love of primacy — which the elder identifies as the root of Diotrephes's refusal to receive the apostolic mission. The diagnosis is remarkable: the problem is not heterodox belief or moral lapse but ecclesiastical status-seeking.

v.10's strong verb for Diotrephes's excommunicatory action. Ekballō (literally 'throw out') is the verb used of Jesus driving out demons and throwing traders from the temple. The elder uses strong language to mark Diotrephes's disciplinary action as itself theologically distortive — using ecclesial discipline to protect his own authority rather than the gospel.

v.8's vocation-description for those who support missionaries: they become synergoi (co-workers) not with the missionaries but with 'the truth' itself. The phrase elevates ordinary hospitality to the theological plane — material support of gospel-labor is itself participation in the gospel's forward movement.

ppl.name: The Elder (Author)
ppl.text: The author identifies only as 'the elder' (ho presbyteros) — the same self-designation as 2 John. Early church tradition unanimously identifies him with John the apostle writing late in life from Ephesus. The letter's compressed style, its family language ('children walking in truth'), and its concern with itinerant-missionary support align with John's other late-century writings.

ppl.name: Gaius
ppl.text: A house-church leader whose name was common (other NT Gaii in Rom 16:23, 1 Cor 1:14, Acts 19:29, 20:4) — this one likely distinct from those. Identified four times as 'beloved.' He has shown exemplary hospitality to traveling missionaries; the elder writes to commend him and to provide him a countermeasure to Diotrephes's exclusionary practice.

ppl.name: Diotrephes
ppl.text: A local church leader whose 'love to be first' has led him to refuse the elder's authority, slander the elder and his circle, refuse hospitality to traveling missionaries, and excommunicate those who welcome them. The letter's occasion is largely Diotrephes's conduct; the elder's coming visit (v.10) carries disciplinary intent. One of the NT's clearest examples of church-leadership gone wrong.

ppl.name: Demetrius
ppl.text: Probably the letter's carrier, whose reputation needs vouching in the hostile Diotrephes-controlled context. The elder's threefold attestation ('well spoken of by everyone, by the truth itself, by us') gives Gaius grounds to receive him over Diotrephes's likely objection. Early tradition sometimes identified him with the silversmith Demetrius of Acts 19, but this identification is speculative.

rec.text: Eusebius classifies 3 John among the 'disputed' (antilegomena) writings — accepted by some but questioned by others, partly because of its brevity and private-letter character. The letter's canonical status was secured by the 4th century but never reached the prominence of longer NT writings. Jerome's treatment parallels Eusebius's.

Westcott's classical commentary argued for unity of authorship between 1-2-3 John on stylistic and theological grounds. The 'elder' of 2 and 3 John is the same figure; the shared vocabulary (truth, love, walking, children, abiding) ties the three letters into a single theological voice. This argument has largely held in subsequent scholarship.

rec.who: Judith Lieu, The Second and Third Epistles of John (1986)
rec.text: Lieu's focused study read 3 John as early Christian evidence of conflict between itinerant-charismatic authority (the 'elder' and his missionary circle) and settled-local authority (Diotrephes's house-church). The letter documents a period when the NT's apostolic charism is transitioning into institutional ecclesial structure; Diotrephes's refusal of itinerant authority prefigures the bishop-monarchical ecclesiology of the 2nd century.

rec.who: Colin Kruse, The Letters of John, Pillar (2000)
rec.text: Kruse's evangelical commentary emphasises 3 John as a pastoral case study: the letter shows how apostolic authority operates in hostile conditions — commending, writing personally, announcing a visit, trusting in-person encounter. The pastoral strategy of the elder is not formal discipline-at-a-distance but relational-presence combined with written testimony to the truth.

thread.text: 'Walking in truth' is the Johannine corpus's signature phrase. 2 John 4 and 3 John 3-4 use near-identical formulations; 1 John 1:6-7 develops the same image as 'walking in the light.' The phrase unites ethical conduct with doctrinal fidelity in characteristic Johannine fashion.

Hospitality to traveling missionaries as participation in their work echoes Jesus's 'whoever receives you receives me' (Matt 10:40-42). The theological principle — welcoming gospel-bearers unites the welcomer with the gospel mission — is continuous from Jesus's sending to 3 John's commendation.

Diotrephes's 'loving to be first' inverts Jesus's teaching that 'whoever would be first among you must be your slave.' The NT's servant-leadership principle receives its sharpest negative instance in 3 John — Diotrephes is the type of church leader the Gospels warned against.

'The one who does good is from God; the one who does evil has not seen God' restates 1 John's repeated test: conduct reveals true spiritual condition. The Johannine insistence that right-relation-to-God produces right-doing is compressed here into a single ethical criterion.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVIt gave me great joy when some believers came and testified about your faithfulness to the truth, telling how you continue to walk in it.KJVFor I rejoiced greatly, when the brethren came and testified of the truth that is in thee, even as thou walkest in the truth.NIVI have no greater joy than to hear that my children are walking in the truth.KJVI have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth.NIVIt was for the sake of the Name that they went out, receiving no help from the pagans.KJVBecause that for his name's sake they went forth, taking nothing of the Gentiles.NIVWe ought therefore to show hospitality to such people so that we may work together for the truth.KJVWe therefore ought to receive such, that we might be fellowhelpers to the truth.NIVDear friend, do not imitate what is evil but what is good. Anyone who does what is good is from God. Anyone who does what is evil has not seen God.KJVBeloved, follow not that which is evil, but that which is good. He that doeth good is of God: but he that doeth evil hath not seen God.NIVI hope to see you soon, and we will talk face to face.KJVBut I trust I shall shortly see thee, and we shall speak face to face.NIVPeace to you. The friends here send their greetings. Greet the friends there by name.KJVPeace be to thee. Our friends salute thee. Greet the friends by name.

coaching.tip: Third John is personal — Gaius is named, praised for receiving traveling preachers 'even though they are strangers' (v. 5). The backdrop: Diotrephes (v. 9) is rejecting these same missionaries. Two church members, opposite responses. The letter shows early Christianity was not uniformly harmonious; personality clashes existed from the start.

Diotrephes 'likes to put himself first' (v. 9) and 'does not acknowledge our authority.' Pride creates structural problems: he refuses hospitality, spreads gossip, and expels those who disagree. John's diagnosis: the problem isn't policy but character. Self-exaltation poisons community. Leadership requires self-forgetfulness.

'Whoever does good is from God; whoever does evil has not seen God' (v. 11). This sounds simplistic — but in context, 'good' and 'evil' are specific: Diotrephes's power plays vs. Gaius's generosity. John isn't moralizing generally but judging particular behaviors. Theology shows up in hospitality.
